bids.reasoning: This is the opening offer at the very beginning of the negotiation (t=0.0). I will bid my most preferred value for every issue to establish a strong anchor and claim maximum utility (1.0). I can evaluate the opponent's preferences and find mutually beneficial concessions in later rounds based on their counteroffers.

The negotiation is just beginning (t=0.02), and the opponent has started with a bid that minimizes my utility, alongside an emotional appeal which I will treat as standard bargaining tactics. To keep negotiations moving productively, I will make a minor concession on my lowest-priority issue (`issueA`) while holding firm on the issues that are most important to my utility (`issueD`, `issueC`, and `issueB`).

The negotiation is still in its very early stages (t=0.04). The opponent has chosen an aggressive tactic, repeating their worst-case offer (valueA for all issues) while using emotional appeals. As these messages are non-binding and the time pressure is non-existent, there is no reason to make a massive concession. I will slightly concede step-by-step on my least important issue (issueA) to demonstrate some flexibility while strictly protecting my highest-weighted issues (issueC and issueD).

The opponent is repeating their opening offer (giving me their lowest values for all issues) while using emotional appeals, which I am disregarding as a negotiation tactic. I have been conceding on issueA, my least important issue, and I will now concede issueA fully to their preferred value (valueA) to see if they are willing to reciprocate with concessions on my more important issues (C and D). This maintains a high utility of 0.92 for me early in the negotiation period while signaling a willingness to compromise on low-weight issues.

The current time is very early (t=0.08), but the opponent has remained completely stagnant on their initial offer of all 'valueA', using emotional appeals to justify their inflexibility. I have already fully conceded on my least important issue (issueA) to show goodwill. Since they are not reciprocating, I will proceed cautiously by making only a minor concession on my second least important issue (issueB) down to valueC, while holding firm on my most critical issues (issueC and issueD).

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.1) and issueC and issueD are my most important issues, so I will firmly hold onto valueD for both. The opponent's message claims desperation, but their slow and tiny concessions (only just stepping slightly on issueD to valueB) indicate they have room to move. To avoid repeating my last bid, I will propose a slight variation on issue A and issue B that still maintains a high utility for me, giving them a chance to reciprocate before I concede further.

The opponent's concessions remain minimal and are accompanied by emotional appeals that I am treating as non-binding cheap talk. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.12) and my reservation value has to be protected, I will offer a newly structured proposal that concedes on my lower-weighted issues (A and B) while firmly defending my highest priorities (C and D) to guide the opponent toward a mutually acceptable tradeoff.

The opponent is continuously using emotional appeals to justify offering me low-utility bids, having only slightly conceded on issueD. Since we are very early in the negotiation (t=0.14), I will ignore the "cheap talk" and maintain a firm stance on my most valuable issues (issueC and issueD). I will slowly concede on my lower-weighted issues (issueA and issueB) to offer a slightly lower, but still highly favorable utility of ~0.84, to test if the opponent will start budging on their end.

The opponent is using emotional appeals and pleading language, but these are common negotiation tactics that don't override the numbers. Their bidding history shows they are willing to make minor concessions on issueD and issueC, suggesting they might value issueA and issueB more. Since my least important issues are issueA and issueB, I will concede fully on issueA and partially on issueB, while firmly protecting issueC and issueD to maintain a high utility (>0.80) at this early stage of the negotiation (t=0.16).

The opponent continues to use repetitive emotional appeals to create a false sense of desperation and urgency, but their bids show they are capable of small concessions on issues C and D. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.18), I will safely ignore their "cheap talk" and focus on maximizing my utility. I will concede entirely on issue B (my second lowest priority) to their opening value A, while slightly increasing my demand on issue A to ensure my overall utility stays around 0.81, holding firmly on my most crucial issues (C and D).

We are relatively early in the negotiation (t=0.20), so I need to maintain a strong position particularly on my most valuable issues, issueC and issueD. I am keeping my values for those issues at their maximum while offering a favorable 'valueA' for issueB to test if the opponent prefers concessions there. I am ignoring the opponent's emotional appeals, as they are non-binding cheap talk and contradict their pattern of minimal structural concessions.

My overall goal is to maximize my utility while making small, gradual concessions given that it is still very early in the negotiation (t=0.22). The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals and claim extreme desperation, but looking at their offer history, they are only making token concessions, shifting from 'valueA' to 'valueB' across alternate issues. Because issues C and D hold the highest weight (0.3 and 0.4 respectively) for me, I will firmly hold those at 'valueD' and try adjusting my bids on the lower-weighted issues A and B to offer the opponent 'valueA' where they seem to want it.

The opponent's emotional messages are evaluated purely as a negotiation tactic, and I rely on their actual bidding behavior. Their latest offer introduces a significant concession to `valueC` on my most important issue, `issueD`, while attempting to keep the rest at `valueA`. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.24), I will maintain strong positions on my two most crucial issues (`issueC` and `issueD`) while offering a concession on my least important issue (`issueA: valueA`) and a moderate value on `issueB`, targeting a utility of 0.866.

The opponent has sent multiple emotional messages claiming desperation, but viewing their offers reveals they are only making minor concessions on issues C and D, while remaining fixed on issue A. Since my priorities heavily favor issue D (weight 0.4) and issue C (weight 0.3), I can afford to concede entirely on issue A and issue B to meet their rigid demands there. I will offer their preferred values for A and B while holding firm on my top preferred values for C and D, maintaining a strong utility of 0.76 early in the negotiation.

The opponent has consistently pressed for `valueA` on most issues, but their latest offer introduces `valueC` for `issueC`. Recognizing this as a potential area for compromise, I will match their latest request on `issueC` and fully concede `issueA` to `valueA`, my least important issue. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.28), I will hold strictly firm on `issueB` and especially `issueD` at `valueD` to maintain high overall utility, treating their dramatic messages as non-binding negotiation tactics.

The current time is still relatively early (t=0.3). The opponent's messages rely entirely on emotional appeals and fabricated constraints to elicit concessions, without directly revealing which issues they prioritize. Therefore, I will rely on the bidding history, where they have tested single-issue compromises (like offering valueB or valueC on issueD and issueC). Because my weights on issueC and issueD are highest, I must secure valueD there. I'll concede on issueA (my lowest priority) to valueA and offer partial movement on issueB to valueC, maintaining a strong overall utility while allowing the opponent to gain value where it hurts me least.

The current time is 0.32, meaning we are still in the early-to-mid stages of the negotiation. The opponent is sending numerous emotional appeals asking for concessions, but their actual offers remain stubbornly low in utility for me, consistently keeping issues A and B at my lowest value options, though they flexed slightly on C and D recently. To keep my utility relatively high while trying a new combination, I will propose a moderate concession on issues A and B while holding firm on my most important issues, C and D.

The opponent continues to accompany offers with strong emotional appeals, but their actual bids show only minor concessions. Because time is only 0.34, I will maintain firm anchors on my high-priority issues (issueD and issueC) to secure strong utility. I will concede partially on my lower-priority issues (issueA and issueB) to show a willingness to compromise and gauge which variables matter most to them.

The opponent's consistent stream of emotional appeals is an obvious negotiation tactic and does not reflect actual constraints, so I will ignore their rhetoric and focus strictly on the numbers. Recognizing their recent proposal of `valueC` for `issueC` and `valueA` for `issueA`, I will match these specific values to demonstrate flexibility, given that they belong to my less-weighted issues. Meanwhile, I am holding firm on my most important issue, `issueD`, returning a robust utility of ~0.785.

The opponent is heavily relying on emotional appeals and fabricated urgency, which I will treat as cheap talk. Their offers, however, indicate a willingness to compromise on issueB and issueC, as seen in their recent bids offering valueC for these issues. Since my highest priorities are issueD (0.4) and C (0.3), I will hold firm on issueD and issueC, while conceding on issueA (weight 0.1) and issueB (weight 0.2) to match their recent shifts and find common ground without sacrificing core utility.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals and fabricated desperation without conveying specific constraints about their utility, so I will treat them as cheap talk and rely solely on their bidding patterns. Their latest offers show some flexibility on issues B, C, and D, while they appear strongly anchored to `valueA` on issue A. I will concede completely on issue A (my lowest priority) and test a slight concession on issue D (`valueC`, which they offered in their last turn), while holding firm on my highest-yielding values for issues B and C to maintain an overall utility around 0.81.

I am targeting a utility of approximately 0.81 by fully conceding on my lowest priority issue (issueA) and partial concession on my second-lowest priority issue (issueB). The opponent relies heavily on emotional appeals in their messages while slowly revealing their potential alternatives, making minor concessions to valueB and valueC. Because we are approaching the midway point, I will afford the opponent their preferred values on the two issues that matter least to my utility in exchange for strictly securing my top preferences on my highest weighted issues (issueC and issueD).

The opponent is using repetitive emotional appeals while showing some flexibility on issues A, B, and C in recent rounds (offering valueB and valueC on those). Since the current time fraction is reaching mid-game (0.44), I will concede further on my least important issues (A and B) while slightly dropping my utility on C to valueC. I will maintain my stance on issueD, my most valuable issue, ensuring a balanced utility (>0.73) for myself.

The opponent's messages rely on emotional appeal ("head above water," "desperate"), which I treat as cheap talk. Their offers consistently include `issueA: valueA` and alternate other issues like `issueB` and `issueD`, suggesting they are somewhat flexible there. Since time is approaching the midpoint (t=0.46), I will hold my core utility intact on `issueD` and `issueC`, conceding fully on `issueA` (my least important issue) and floating `valueD` for `issueB` while offering `valueC` on `issueC` to see if they prefer this configuration, securing a high utility for me (~0.84) while extending a different compromise than my previous turn.

We are nearing the halfway mark of the negotiation. The opponent's emotive messages suggest high pressure, but their actual bidding history shows flexibility on issues A, B, and C (having offered `valueC` for each of them recently). I will hold firm on issueD (my highest priority) but concede on all other issues by offering `valueC` across the board, matching proposals they have demonstrated a willingness to accept.

We are exactly at the halfway point (t=0.5). The opponent's message history consists exclusively of emotional appeals, which I will treat as cheap talk and ignore in favor of reading their actual bidding behavior. In recent offers, the opponent has shown a willingness to move to 'valueB' and 'valueC' on issueC and issueB. To advance the negotiation, I will propose a compromise that gives them a favorable concession on my low-weight issues (issueA and issueB), while maintaining 'valueC' on issueC and firmly holding onto 'valueD' on my highest-priority issue, issueD, targeting a utility of around 0.76.

The opponent continues to use emotional appeals ("pleading", "devastating") as cheap talk, while their offers reveal flexibility on issueB (they just offered issueB: valueD at t=0.52). Since my utility for issueB: valueD is high but my weight for it is secondary to issueD and issueC, I will accept their concession on issueB. In exchange, I can hold firm on issueD (my highest priority) while keeping issueA at valueA (their apparent priority) and offering a slight concession on issueC to valueB to move closer to an agreement at the midpoint.

The opponent's emotional appeals are treated as cheap talk. I am holding firm on my most important issue (issueD), maximizing its value. I am matching the opponent's previous willingness to explore valueC for both issueB and issueC, while keeping issueA at a moderate concession. This aligns with a mid-game strategy of probing different combinations of their past concessions while maintaining an overall utility near 0.8 for myself.

The opponent's messages are filled with emotional appeals and exaggerated claims of desperation, which I will discard as cheap talk tactics. Looking at their actual historical bids, they've shown significant flexibility on `issueB` (recently dropping to valueD at t=0.52) and `issueC`. Therefore, I will secure my high utility on my most heavily weighted issues (`issueD` and `issueB`/`issueC`) while making a modest concession on `issueA`, maintaining an overall utility over 0.85 as we are just past the halfway mark.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals and fake urgency to pressure me into accepting their terms, which I will disregard as cheap talk. In their recent offers, they proposed 'valueD' on 'issueB' and 'valueB' on 'issueD', showing a willingness to compromise. I will give them 'valueA' on 'issueA', an issue they clearly prioritize and where my weight is low, while securing 'valueD' on 'issueB' (which they just offered). I will hold firm near my top utilities for my most important issues, 'issueC' and 'issueD'.

The current time is at 60% of the deadline, and the opponent relies heavily on emotional appeals while slightly varying their offers. They have recently proposed `valueD` for `issueB` and `valueB` for `issueC`, demonstrating flexibility there while consistently insisting on `valueA` for `issueA`. To move toward an agreement, I will match their latest position on `issueA`, `issueB`, and `issueC`, making a substantial concession that yields a solid 0.76 utility, but I am holding firm on `issueD` (`valueD`), which is my most critical priority.

The opponent's latest offer gives us values we can accept on issues A, B, and C (valueA, valueC, and valueC respectively). However, they are still demanding a large concession on issueD, which is my most highly weighted issue. I will align entirely with their last offer on issues A, B, and C, effectively giving them what they requested there, while maintaining valueD on issueD to keep my utility solid at ~0.78.

The opponent is relying heavily on emotional appeals to rush an agreement, repeatedly citing desperation. However, I must disregard these tactics and analyze their underlying concessions. Since my highest priority issues are issueD and issueC, I will firmly hold issueD at valueD and maintain issueC at valueC while testing a varied combination on the lower-weight issues to probe for acceptable trade-offs without compromising my core utility.

The opponent's last offer includes valueD for issueB and valueB for issueC, which are favorable concessions. Since they employ heavily emotionally-charged messaging that I will treat as standard negotiation tactics, I rely purely on their offer patterns. To bring us closer to an agreement while protecting my highest priority, I will accept their exact terms on issues A, B, and C, but hold firm on issueD (valueD). This secures a solid utility of 0.761 for me while demonstrating significant flexibility.

The opponent's most recent offer shows significant concession across issues B, C, and D, moving to valueC for all of them while keeping A at valueA. Their messages are full of emotional appeals which I treat as non-binding cheap talk. Since we are around t=0.68, I am targeting a utility of roughly 0.78 by accepting their terms for issues A, B, and C while holding firm on issue D since it is my highest priority issue.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals and claims of distress, which I am disregarding as cheap talk. Focusing on the actual bid history, at t=0.68 our offers were nearly identical (theirs: A:A, B:C, C:C, D:C; mine: A:A, B:C, C:C, D:D). At t=0.70, they slightly pivoted, possibly exploring other tradeoffs. Given the current time fraction, I will stand firm on issue D (my highest weight) where I receive my maximum utility, while matching their previously favorable values for A, B, and C to maintain a high-utility near-agreement.

I am targeting a utility of 0.733 to demonstrate a steady concession rate as the deadline approaches (t=0.72). I noticed that the opponent frequently asks for valueA or valueB on issueB. By yielding from valueC to valueB on issueB while retaining my highest utility value on my most important issue (issueD), I hope to reach a favorable compromise. I am ignoring the opponent's emotional appeals of "desperation" and focusing strictly on the bidding pattern.
